# Metro Integrated Planning System - Progress Log

## Session: 2025-12-15 (Production App Migration)

### Migration to Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS

**Decision:** Migrate validated POC (URGENT_POC/canvas.html) to production app (apps/canvas).

**Tech Stack:**
- Vite 5.x (build tool)
- React 18.3.1 (required for BlueprintJS 6)
- BlueprintJS 6.1.0 (UI components from Palantir)
- @react-three/fiber 8.x (pairs with React 18)
- @react-three/drei 9.x (R3F helpers)
- three 0.170.x (WebGL)

**Key Constraint:** React 18 + R3F 8.x pairing is critical. BlueprintJS 6 requires React 18.

**Changes:**
- Updated features.json:
  - Changed product entry to apps/canvas
  - Archived POC (URGENT_POC/canvas.html)
  - Moved F016-F028 to deferred archive
  - Added F101-F117 for production app features
  - Added "canvas" category
- Updated CLAUDE.md:
  - Tech Stack section with production stack
  - Quick Commands for apps/canvas
  - Project Structure for new layout
  - Marked URGENT_POC as archived
- Updated docs/README.md with production app reference
- Updated docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md with R3F/BlueprintJS details
- Updated agent_docs/architecture.md:
  - New tech choices (Vite, React 18, BlueprintJS)
  - Version compatibility matrix
  - R3F version pairing warning

**Next:** Create apps/canvas skeleton (F101)

---

## Session: 2025-12-15 (Harness Consolidation)

### Documentation Update - canvas.html as Single Product

**Changes:**
- Redesigned `features.json` with canvas.html as the single product entry point
- Consolidated all features to target canvas.html (F001-F028)
- Updated `CLAUDE.md` URGENT_POC section to reflect canvas.html focus
- Updated `docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md` implementation details
- Updated `docs/README.md` with current implementation status

**New Feature Structure:**
- F001-F015: Core canvas features (PASS) - pan, zoom, LOD, sidebar, timeline
- F016-F017: Scroll modifiers (PENDING) - Shift+Scroll, Ctrl+Scroll
- F018-F020: Interactions (PENDING) - hover, click, tooltip
- F021-F022: Accessibility (PENDING) - keyboard nav, ARIA
- F023-F028: Advanced features (PENDING) - dependencies, minimap, shortcuts

**Archive:**
- Legacy HTML files (index.html, macro.html, meso.html, micro.html, layout.html) kept as visual references only
- These files are NOT the product - canvas.html is the single entry point

**Rationale:**
- The semantic zoom UX requires a single interactive canvas
- Multiple static HTML files were prototypes to validate individual layer designs
- canvas.html integrates all layers with real pan/zoom interaction

---

## Session: 2025-12-15 (F017 Pan/Zoom Interactivity)

### F017: Pan/Zoom Interactivity - COMPLETE

**Implemented:**
- Created `URGENT_POC/canvas.html` with full semantic zoom
- Drag-to-pan: Click and drag to move the viewport
- Scroll-to-zoom: Mouse wheel zooms toward cursor position
- Double-click to zoom in 2x at click location
- Zoom controls (+/- buttons) and Reset View button
- LOD transitions working correctly:
  - **MACRO** (zoom < 0.5x): Heatmap with yellow-orange-red gradient
  - **MESO** (zoom 0.5-2x): DNA strips with colored task blocks
  - **MICRO** (zoom > 2x): Detailed task cards with labels and grid
- Timeline header adapts granularity (years → months → weeks → days)
- Sidebar shows visible train IDs synced with viewport
- LOD indicator in header shows current zoom level

**Visual Tests (Playwright MCP):**
- ✅ MESO view: Task blocks visible, conflict indicators working
- ✅ MACRO view: Heatmap rendering, all 50 trains visible
- ✅ MICRO view: Grid, task cards with labels
- ✅ No console errors

**Next feature:** F018 (Click/hover interactions)

---

## Session: 2025-12-15 (F016-BUG Verification)

### F016-BUG: layout.html JS rendering issue - RESOLVED

**Investigation:**
- Visual test via Playwright MCP shows page renders correctly
- Timeline header (Oct 1-8) displays properly
- All task blocks visible (original/red, resolved/blue, unchanged/gray)
- Conflict zone with hatched pattern renders correctly
- Resolution arrow SVG displays properly
- Legend visible and styled correctly
- No console errors

**Conclusion:** The reported "blank screen" issue was a false positive or was resolved in a prior session. Marking F016-BUG as passes:true.

**Next feature:** F017 (Pan/zoom interactivity)

---

## Session: 2025-12-15 (Claude Code Hooks)

### Implemented Harness Enforcement via Hooks

Based on:
- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- [Claude Code Hooks Guide](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/hooks-guide)

**Hooks Created:**
- [x] `session-start.sh` (SessionStart) - Harness protocol reminders + package versions
- [x] `protect-package-json.sh` (PreToolUse) - BLOCKS manual package.json edits
- [x] `after-write.sh` (PostToolUse) - Quick feedback after file edits
- [x] `task-type-detector.py` (UserPromptSubmit) - Task-type aware reminders
- [x] `end-of-turn-check.sh` (Stop) - Quality gate at end of turn

**Architecture:**
```
PreToolUse  → BLOCKING (prevent bad actions)
PostToolUse → FAST (quick feedback)
Stop        → QUALITY GATE (typecheck, visual test reminders)
```

**Commit:** 85485f8

## Recurring Issues & Solutions

### Issue: R3F or Three.js import errors
**Solution**: Use `bun add` to install packages, never edit package.json manually

### Issue: Astro hydration issues with React
**Solution**: Always use `client:only="react"` for R3F components

### Issue: Shader compilation errors
**Solution**: Check GLSL syntax, verify uniform names match

### Issue: TypeScript errors with toolkit state machine
**Solution**: Use usePlanningSelector() instead of usePlanning() for type safety

{
  "project": "Metro Integrated Planning System (MMS)",
  "version": "0.2.0",
  "lastUpdated": "2025-12-15",
  "product": {
    "entry": "apps/canvas",
    "description": "Production React app with semantic zoom (Vite + React 18 + R3F + BlueprintJS 6)"
  },
  "poc": {
    "entry": "URGENT_POC/canvas.html",
    "description": "Archived POC - validated prototype, migrating to production app",
    "status": "archived"
  },
  "features": [
    {
      "id": "F001",
      "category": "core",
      "description": "Canvas renders and initializes without errors",
      "steps": [
        "Open URGENT_POC/canvas.html in browser",
        "Canvas displays with header, sidebar, and main area",
        "No JavaScript errors in console",
        "Mock data generates 50 trains with 10 years of tasks"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "notes": "Foundation - canvas.html is the single product entry point"
    },
    {
      "id": "F002",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Pan navigation with drag",
      "steps": [
        "Click and drag on canvas to pan",
        "Cursor changes to grabbing while dragging",
        "Pan works in all directions (X and Y)",
        "Sidebar and timeline header sync with pan position"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F001",
      "notes": "Implemented with mousedown/mousemove/mouseup events"
    },
    {
      "id": "F003",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Zoom navigation with scroll wheel",
      "steps": [
        "Scroll up to zoom in, scroll down to zoom out",
        "Zoom centers on mouse cursor position",
        "Zoom respects MIN_ZOOM (0.1x) and MAX_ZOOM (5x) limits",
        "Zoom level indicator updates in header"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F001",
      "notes": "Zoom toward mouse position for natural feel"
    },
    {
      "id": "F004",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Double-click zoom",
      "steps": [
        "Double-click zooms in 2x at click location",
        "Works at any current zoom level (respects max)",
        "Animation is smooth (no jarring jump)"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Quick zoom in gesture"
    },
    {
      "id": "F005",
      "category": "navigation",
      "description": "Zoom button controls",
      "steps": [
        "Plus button zooms in by 1.5x",
        "Minus button zooms out by 1.5x",
        "Reset button returns to zoom 1.0 and pan origin",
        "Buttons zoom toward center of viewport"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Alternative for users without scroll wheel"
    },
    {
      "id": "F006",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "Macro layer renders at zoom < 0.5x",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom out below 0.5x to trigger Macro view",
        "Heatmap displays with Yellow-Orange-Red gradient",
        "Cell density reflects task count per month/train",
        "LOD indicator shows 'MACRO' with yellow badge"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Strategic 10-20 year overview"
    },
    {
      "id": "F007",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "Meso layer renders at zoom 0.5x-2x",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom to 0.5x-2x range to trigger Meso view",
        "Task blocks render as colored rectangles (DNA strips)",
        "Alternating row backgrounds for readability",
        "LOD indicator shows 'MESO' with blue badge"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Tactical 2-5 year view with task blocks"
    },
    {
      "id": "F008",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "Micro layer renders at zoom > 2x",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom above 2x to trigger Micro view",
        "Task cards render with shadows and rounded corners",
        "Grid lines show days (vertical) and trains (horizontal)",
        "Task type labels visible when card is wide enough",
        "LOD indicator shows 'MICRO' with green badge"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F003",
      "notes": "Operational 4-8 week view with detailed cards"
    },
    {
      "id": "F009",
      "category": "lod",
      "description": "LOD transitions are smooth",
      "steps": [
        "Zoom from Macro to Meso - no flickering",
        "Zoom from Meso to Micro - no flickering",
        "State persists correctly across transitions",
        "Performance stays smooth during transition"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F006",
      "notes": "Critical for Google Earth-like UX feel"
    },
    {
      "id": "F010",
      "category": "ui",
      "description": "Sidebar displays train IDs synced with canvas",
      "steps": [
        "Train IDs visible in left sidebar",
        "IDs update as canvas pans vertically",
        "Row height matches canvas zoom level",
        "Only visible trains are rendered (virtual scroll)"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F002",
      "notes": "Sidebar syncs with panY position"
    },
    {
      "id": "F011",
      "category": "ui",
      "description": "Timeline header displays dates synced with canvas",
      "steps": [
        "Timeline labels update as canvas pans horizontally",
        "Granularity changes based on zoom (years/months/weeks/days)",
        "Labels are readable and don't overlap",
        "Timeline syncs with panX position"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F002",
      "notes": "Adaptive granularity: years at low zoom, days at high zoom"
    },
    {
      "id": "F012",
      "category": "visual",
      "description": "Conflict indicators visible on tasks",
      "steps": [
        "Tasks with conflicts show red border/glow",
        "Conflicts visible in Meso layer (red stroke)",
        "Conflicts visible in Micro layer (red glow shadow)",
        "~5% of tasks have conflict flag for testing"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F007",
      "notes": "Red highlight draws attention to scheduling issues"
    },
    {
      "id": "F013",
      "category": "visual",
      "description": "Task colors match design specifications",
      "steps": [
        "Maintenance tasks: Blue gradient (#3b82f6 to #2563eb)",
        "Overhaul tasks: Orange gradient (#f59e0b to #d97706)",
        "Inspection tasks: Gray gradient (#9ca3af to #6b7280)",
        "Gradients render correctly at all zoom levels"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F007",
      "notes": "Color coding matches docs/UX_SPECIFICATION.md"
    },
    {
      "id": "F014",
      "category": "ux",
      "description": "Onboarding instructions display and auto-hide",
      "steps": [
        "Instructions tooltip visible on page load",
        "Shows: 'Scroll to zoom - Drag to pan - Double-click to zoom in'",
        "Auto-fades after 5 seconds",
        "Non-intrusive (pointer-events: none)"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F001",
      "notes": "Progressive disclosure - teach on first use"
    },
    {
      "id": "F015",
      "category": "ux",
      "description": "LOD legend explains zoom levels",
      "steps": [
        "Legend panel visible in bottom-left corner",
        "Shows Macro (yellow), Meso (blue), Micro (green) colors",
        "Describes zoom thresholds (<0.5x, 0.5-2x, >2x)",
        "Helps users understand semantic zoom concept"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": "F001",
      "notes": "Educational UI element"
    },
    {
      "id": "F101",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Create Vite + React 18 + BlueprintJS app skeleton",
      "steps": [
        "Create apps/canvas directory with Vite config",
        "Install React 18.3.1, BlueprintJS 6.1.0",
        "Create basic App.tsx with BlueprintJS Navbar",
        "App runs on localhost:5173 without errors",
        "BlueprintJS styles load correctly"
      ],
      "passes": true,
      "blockedBy": null,
      "notes": "Foundation for production app - React 18 required for BlueprintJS 6"
    },
    {
      "id": "F102",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Add R3F Canvas with OrthographicCamera",
      "steps": [
        "Install @react-three/fiber and @react-three/drei",
        "Create InfiniteCanvas component with R3F Canvas",
        "Setup OrthographicCamera for 2D-like view",
        "Canvas fills available space below navbar",
        "No WebGL errors in console"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F101",
      "notes": "R3F 8.x compatible with React 18"
    },
    {
      "id": "F103",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Basic BlueprintJS layout (Navbar, sidebar placeholder)",
      "steps": [
        "Navbar with app title and zoom indicator",
        "Left sidebar container for train IDs",
        "Main area for R3F canvas",
        "Layout uses flexbox, fills viewport",
        "Responsive to window resize"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F102",
      "notes": "BlueprintJS Card, Navbar, Button components"
    },
    {
      "id": "F104",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Data types and mock generator",
      "steps": [
        "Create Train, Task, Config TypeScript interfaces",
        "Port generateData() from canvas.html",
        "Generate 50 trains with 10 years of tasks",
        "~5% tasks have conflict flag",
        "Data available via React context or store"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F101",
      "notes": "Port logic directly from POC canvas.html"
    },
    {
      "id": "F105",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "MesoLayer - InstancedMesh task blocks",
      "steps": [
        "Create MesoLayer component using InstancedMesh",
        "Render task blocks as colored rectangles",
        "Colors: blue (maintenance), orange (overhaul), gray (inspection)",
        "Alternating row backgrounds for readability",
        "Tasks visible at default zoom (1.0x)"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F104",
      "notes": "Default view - DNA-like pattern"
    },
    {
      "id": "F106",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Pan and Zoom camera controls",
      "steps": [
        "Drag to pan (mousedown + mousemove)",
        "Scroll to zoom (wheel event)",
        "Zoom centers on mouse cursor",
        "Respect MIN_ZOOM (0.1) and MAX_ZOOM (5.0)",
        "Cursor changes to grab/grabbing"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F102",
      "notes": "Port handleWheel, handleMouseDown/Move/Up from POC"
    },
    {
      "id": "F107",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "LOD state management",
      "steps": [
        "Create useViewport hook with zoom, panX, panY state",
        "Compute LOD from zoom: MACRO (<0.5), MESO (0.5-2), MICRO (>2)",
        "LOD changes trigger layer visibility",
        "State persists across renders",
        "Export state for UI components"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F106",
      "notes": "Central state for viewport management"
    },
    {
      "id": "F108",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "MacroLayer - Heatmap cells",
      "steps": [
        "Create MacroLayer component",
        "Render heatmap cells based on task density",
        "Color gradient: yellow -> orange -> red",
        "Only visible when zoom < 0.5x",
        "Smooth transition from MesoLayer"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F107",
      "notes": "Strategic 10-20 year view"
    },
    {
      "id": "F109",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "MicroLayer - Html task cards with labels",
      "steps": [
        "Create MicroLayer using drei Html component",
        "Render detailed task cards with shadows",
        "Show task type label when card wide enough",
        "Grid lines for days and trains",
        "Only visible when zoom > 2x"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F107",
      "notes": "Operational 4-8 week view"
    },
    {
      "id": "F110",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Smooth LOD transitions",
      "steps": [
        "Transition between layers without flickering",
        "No visual artifacts during zoom",
        "State persists across LOD changes",
        "Performance stays smooth",
        "Compare with POC behavior"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F108",
      "notes": "Critical for Google Earth-like UX"
    },
    {
      "id": "F111",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "TrainSidebar synced with camera Y",
      "steps": [
        "Create TrainSidebar component with BlueprintJS Card",
        "Display train IDs in list",
        "Sync position with camera panY",
        "Row height scales with zoom",
        "Virtual scroll for performance"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F107",
      "notes": "BlueprintJS Menu or custom list"
    },
    {
      "id": "F112",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Timeline header with adaptive granularity",
      "steps": [
        "Create TimelineHeader component",
        "Show date labels synced with panX",
        "Adapt granularity: years -> months -> weeks -> days",
        "Labels don't overlap",
        "Positioned above canvas"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F107",
      "notes": "Port renderTimelineHeader from POC"
    },
    {
      "id": "F113",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "ZoomControls and LODIndicator in Navbar",
      "steps": [
        "Add zoom level text (e.g., '1.00x') to Navbar",
        "Add LOD indicator Tag (MACRO/MESO/MICRO)",
        "Add ButtonGroup with +/- zoom buttons",
        "Add Reset button",
        "Style with BlueprintJS Intent colors"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F107",
      "notes": "BlueprintJS Tag, Button, ButtonGroup"
    },
    {
      "id": "F114",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Conflict indicators (Intent.DANGER styling)",
      "steps": [
        "Tasks with conflict flag show red styling",
        "Use BlueprintJS Intent.DANGER color (#cd4246)",
        "Visible in Meso and Micro layers",
        "Red glow/border effect",
        "~5% of tasks show conflict"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F105",
      "notes": "Port conflict styling from POC"
    },
    {
      "id": "F115",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Instructions Toaster (auto-dismiss)",
      "steps": [
        "Show Toaster on first load",
        "Message: 'Scroll to zoom - Drag to pan'",
        "Auto-dismiss after 5 seconds",
        "Use BlueprintJS Toaster component",
        "Non-blocking (pointer-events: none)"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F103",
      "notes": "BlueprintJS OverlayToaster"
    },
    {
      "id": "F116",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Performance optimization (60fps)",
      "steps": [
        "Profile with Chrome DevTools",
        "Maintain 60fps during pan/zoom",
        "No memory leaks on extended use",
        "InstancedMesh optimizations",
        "Frustum culling for off-screen tasks"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F110",
      "notes": "Critical for production quality"
    },
    {
      "id": "F117",
      "category": "canvas",
      "description": "Full visual parity with canvas.html POC",
      "steps": [
        "Compare screenshots with POC",
        "All 3 LOD layers match visually",
        "Pan/zoom behavior identical",
        "Sidebar and timeline sync correctly",
        "Conflict indicators match"
      ],
      "passes": false,
      "blockedBy": "F116",
      "notes": "Final validation - POC as reference"
    }
  ],

content.content:
1→# Metro Integrated Planning System (Monorepo)
     2→
     3→High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style).
     4→
     5→## Commands
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bun i              # Install dependencies
     9→bun run dev        # Start development server
    10→bun run build      # Production build
    11→bun run typecheck  # Type check all packages
    12→bun run lint       # Lint all packages
    13→bun run test       # Run tests
    14→```
    15→
    16→## Tech Stack
    17→
    18→- **Runtime:** Bun
    19→- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
    20→- **App Framework:** Astro (Static/SPA, client-only React)
    21→- **Frontend:** React 19
    22→- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber, @react-three/drei, three-stdlib
    23→- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)
    24→- **Styling:** Tailwind CSS v4
    25→
    26→## Project Structure
    27→
    28→```
    29→system/
    30→├── apps/system/          # Main Astro application
    31→├── packages/
    32→│   ├── domain/           # Business logic, entities, data generators
    33→│   ├── gl-engine/        # WebGL/R3F components, shaders, layers
    34→│   ├── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities
    35→│   └── ui/               # Atomic UI components
    36→└── design/               # Visual reference specifications (Pixel-Perfect Target)
    37→```
    38→
    39→## Visual References (design/)
    40→
    41→The `design/` directory contains the visual specifications that this system must replicate. See [design/README.md](./design/README.md) for detailed mapping between images and implementation layers.
    42→
    43→| Image | View | Description |
    44→|-------|------|-------------|
    45→| screen-1.png | Macro | Strategic 10-20 year heatmap overview |
    46→| screen-2.png | Meso | Tactical DNA-like allocation strips |
    47→| screen-3.jpg | Micro | Operational task cards with dependencies |
    48→| screen-4.png | Meso | Dense diagonal allocation pattern |
    49→| screen-5.png | Meso | Color-coded resource allocation |
    50→| screen-6.png | Micro | Task details with conflict indicators |
    51→| screen-7.png | UI | Header/sidebar layout reference |
    52→
    53→## LOD System (Level of Detail)
    54→
    55→The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:
    56→
    57→- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
    58→- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
    59→- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves
    60→
    61→## Documentation
    62→
    63→- `docs/` - Domain documentation (requirements, UX specs, research)
    64→- `agent_docs/` - Technical documentation for AI agents
    65→- `design/` - Visual reference specifications
    66→
    67→---
    68→
    69→# Harness Protocol for Long-Running AI Agents
    70→
    71→## What is a Harness?
    72→
    73→A **harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work effectively on complex tasks that span multiple sessions. Created by **Anthropic** as part of the Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK), the harness addresses a fundamental challenge:
    74→
    75→> "The core challenge of long-running agents is that they must work in discrete sessions, and each new session begins with no memory of what came before."
    76→
    77→The harness acts as a **coordination layer** wrapped around ordinary Claude sessions. It tracks files, tasks, and test results across hours of execution, spinning up fresh conversations whenever one thread gets too bloated to stay coherent.
    78→
    79→## Why Anthropic Created the Harness
    80→
    81→After more than six months shipping updates to Claude Code, Anthropic solved hard problems:
    82→- How agents should manage **memory across long-running tasks**
    83→- How to handle **permission systems** that balance autonomy with user control
    84→- How to **coordinate subagents** working toward a shared goal
    85→
    86→The key insight was finding a way for agents to quickly understand the state of work when starting with a fresh context window.
    87→
    88→## Core Architecture
    89→
    90→```
    91→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    92→│                    Harness Framework                         │
    93→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
    94→│                                                              │
    95→│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
    96→│  │  Initializer │ → │    Coding    │ → │    Coding    │  │
    97→│  │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │  │
    98→│  │  (Session 1) │    │  (Session 2) │    │  (Session N) │  │
    99→│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
   100→│         │                   │                   │           │
   101→│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
   102→│                             │                               │
   103→│                             ▼                               │
   104→│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
   105→│  │              Shared State Files                       │  │
   106→│  │  • claude-progress.txt  (session history)            │  │
   107→│  │  • features.json        (source of truth)            │  │
   108→│  │  • CLAUDE.md            (project context)            │  │
   109→│  │  • git history          (code changes)               │  │
   110→│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
   111→│                                                              │
   112→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
   113→```
   114→
   115→## Two-Agent Architecture
   116→
   117→Anthropic recommends a **two-fold approach**:
   118→
   119→### 1. Initializer Agent (First Session)
   120→- Sets up the initial environment
   121→- Creates the harness files (features.json, claude-progress.txt)
   122→- Establishes project structure
   123→- Runs on a specialized prompt optimized for setup
   124→
   125→### 2. Coding Agent (Subsequent Sessions)
   126→- Makes incremental progress on features
   127→- Leaves artifacts for the next session
   128→- Maintains clean code suitable for merging
   129→- Commits progress to git with descriptive messages
   130→
   131→## Core Files
   132→
   133→| File | Purpose |
   134→|------|---------|
   135→| `CLAUDE.md` | Project context, instructions, and conventions |
   136→| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history and current state |
   137→| `features.json` | Feature requirements with pass/fail status (source of truth) |
   138→| `agent_docs/` | Additional documentation for the agent |
   139→| `init.sh` | Script to start dev server and run basic tests |
   140→| `CLAUDE_NOTES.md` | Learnings and gotchas for future sessions |
   141→
   142→## The Work Loop
   143→
   144→When a session starts, the agent follows this loop:
   145→
   146→```
   147→1. READ claude-progress.txt
   148→   → Understand current state and what was done before
   149→
   150→2. READ features.json
   151→   → Identify next feature to implement (passes: false)
   152→
   153→3. RUN init.sh
   154→   → Start dev server, verify app is in working state
   155→
   156→4. IMPLEMENT one feature at a time
   157→   → Focus on single feature, don't try to do everything
   158→
   159→5. TEST the feature
   160→   → Verify it works as specified in steps
   161→
   162→6. COMMIT changes
   163→   → Granular commits with descriptive messages
   164→
   165→7. UPDATE features.json
   166→   → Mark feature as passes: true
   167→
   168→8. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
   169→   → Log what was done for next session
   170→
   171→9. REPEAT or STOP
   172→   → Continue to next feature or end session
   173→```
   174→
   175→## Critical AI Limitations & Mitigations
   176→
   177→> **IMPORTANT**: AI agents have fundamental limitations that this harness addresses.
   178→
   179→### The Blind Spot Problem
   180→
   181→**AI cannot see what it creates.** When building UI/apps, the AI:
   182→- Cannot see the rendered output
   183→- Often assumes blank screens are "working" because code compiled
   184→- Marks features as complete based on code logic, not visual verification
   185→- May claim success when the app shows nothing or errors
   186→
   187→**Solution**: Mandatory visual testing with MCP after every feature.
   188→
   189→### The Stale Knowledge Problem
   190→
   191→**AI training data is outdated.** Claude's knowledge cutoff means:
   192→- Library APIs may have changed
   193→- Configuration patterns may be deprecated
   194→- New versions have different syntax
   195→- Best practices may have evolved
   196→
   197→**Solution**: Always check the current date and search for updated documentation.
   198→
   199→### The Guessing Problem
   200→
   201→**AI tends to guess instead of research.** When stuck, AI will:
   202→- Try random solutions repeatedly
   203→- Waste context on failed attempts
   204→- Not realize when it's going in circles
   205→
   206→**Solution**: Mandatory research protocol for non-obvious errors.
   207→
   208→---
   209→
   210→## Best Practices
   211→
   212→### 1. One Feature at a Time
   213→Don't attempt to complete everything at once. Focus on a single feature, test it, commit it, then move to the next.
   214→
   215→### 2. Clean State Between Sessions
   216→Leave the environment in a clean state:
   217→- No major bugs
   218→- Well-documented code
   219→- Orderly structure
   220→- Tests passing
   221→
   222→### 3. features.json is Source of Truth
   223→Never declare the project complete without all features passing. The `passes` field should only change from `false` to `true` after verification.
   224→
   225→### 4. Commit After Each Feature
   226→Maintain granular git history. Each feature should have its own commit(s).
   227→
   228→### 5. Research Before Guessing
   229→When encountering errors, classify them:
   230→- **Simple errors** (typos, syntax): Fix directly
   231→- **Complex errors** (runtime, config): Research online first
   232→
   233→### 6. Test Before Marking Complete
   234→Always verify the feature works before marking `passes: true`.
   235→
   236→### 7. ALWAYS Check Current Date
   237→Before starting work, verify the current date. **Do NOT assume you are in the year of your training data.** Use the system date to ensure you search for current documentation.
   238→
   239→### 8. Install Dependencies via Commands
   240→**NEVER manually edit package.json/Cargo.toml/requirements.txt to add dependencies.**
   241→
   242→```bash
   243→# CORRECT - Let package manager resolve versions
   244→bun add react
   245→npm install express
   246→pip install requests
   247→cargo add serde
   248→
   249→# WRONG - Manual version specification
   250→# Editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"  <- DON'T DO THIS
   251→```
   252→
   253→Why: Package managers install the latest stable compatible versions and handle peer dependencies correctly.
   254→
   255→---
   256→
   257→## MCP Visual Testing (MANDATORY)
   258→
   259→### Why Visual Testing is Required
   260→
   261→AI agents **cannot see rendered UI**. This creates a critical gap:
   262→
   263→```
   264→What AI knows:          What actually happens:
   265→✓ Code compiled         ✗ Blank white screen
   266→✓ No TypeScript errors  ✗ React error boundary
   267→✓ Tests passed          ✗ Component doesn't render
   268→✓ Server running        ✗ CSS completely broken
   269→```
   270→
   271→**Without visual verification, AI will mark broken features as complete.**
   272→
   273→### Visual Testing Protocol
   274→
   275→**See CLAUDE.md section "MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)"** for the complete protocol.
   276→
   277→Summary: Use **Playwright MCP** (headless, does not steal focus):
   278→
   279→```
   280→1. mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="..."
   281→2. mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
   282→3. mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="..."
   283→4. Analyze screenshot
   284→5. mcp__playwright__browser_close
   285→```
   286→
   287→---
   288→
   289→## Error Research Protocol
   290→
   291→### When to Research vs. Fix Directly
   292→
   293→| Error Type | Example | Action |
   294→|------------|---------|--------|
   295→| **Obvious** | Typo in variable name | Fix directly |
   296→| **Syntax** | Missing semicolon | Fix directly |
   297→| **Import** | Wrong import path | Fix directly |
   298→| **Config** | Webpack/Vite config | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   299→| **Runtime** | Module not found | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   300→| **Library** | API changed | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   301→| **Unknown** | Cryptic error | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
   302→
   303→### The STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY Method
   304→
   305→When encountering non-obvious errors:
   306→
   307→```
   308→1. STOP
   309→   - Do NOT try random solutions
   310→   - Do NOT keep making changes hoping something works
   311→   - Recognize when you're stuck
   312→
   313→2. SEARCH
   314→   - WebSearch: "[error message] + [technology] + [current year]"
   315→   - WebFetch: Read official documentation
   316→   - Include the CURRENT YEAR in searches (not your training year)
   317→
   318→3. UNDERSTAND
   319→   - Read the search results carefully
   320→   - Identify the ROOT CAUSE
   321→   - Understand WHY the error occurs
   322→
   323→4. APPLY
   324→   - Implement the CORRECT solution
   325→   - Not a workaround or hack
   326→   - The solution from documentation
   327→
   328→5. DOCUMENT
   329→   - Add to CLAUDE_NOTES.md if it's a recurring issue
   330→   - Note in claude-progress.txt
   331→```
   332→
   333→### Example: Research Flow
   334→
   335→```
   336→Error: "Module '@tauri-apps/api/fs' not found"
   337→
   338→❌ WRONG approach:
   339→   → Try different import paths randomly
   340→   → Downgrade versions blindly
   341→   → Copy code from training data (outdated)
   342→
   343→✅ CORRECT approach:
   344→   1. STOP - This is a config/library error
   345→   2. SEARCH - "tauri 2.0 fs module not found 2025"
   346→   3. UNDERSTAND - Tauri 2.0 moved fs to plugin system
   347→   4. APPLY - Install @tauri-apps/plugin-fs, update imports
   348→   5. DOCUMENT - Note in CLAUDE_NOTES.md
   349→```
   350→
   351→---
   352→
   353→## Session Startup Sequence
   354→
   355→Every coding agent session should begin with:
   356→
   357→```bash
   358→# 1. Confirm working directory
   359→pwd
   360→
   361→# 2. Read progress to understand state
   362→cat claude-progress.txt
   363→
   364→# 3. Check recent git history
   365→git log --oneline -10
   366→
   367→# 4. Read feature requirements
   368→cat features.json
   369→
   370→# 5. Start development server
   371→./init.sh
   372→
   373→# 6. Verify app is working
   374→# (run basic tests or visual check)
   375→
   376→# 7. Begin work on next feature
   377→```
   378→
   379→## Context Management
   380→
   381→The Claude Agent SDK implements automatic **compaction** when context limit approaches. However, you can help by:
   382→
   383→- Using `/clear` between unrelated tasks
   384→- Delegating research to subagents
   385→- Keeping CLAUDE.md concise
   386→- Writing clear, focused commit messages
   387→
   388→## Results
   389→
   390→Using this harness approach, Anthropic has observed:
   391→- Claude maintaining focus for **30+ hours** on complex tasks
   392→- Agents completing multi-week projects across sessions
   393→- Consistent quality without context degradation
   394→
   395→## Official Sources
   396→
   397→- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
   398→- [Building agents with the Claude Agent SDK](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-agents-with-the-claude-agent-sdk)
   399→- [Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)

# Metro Integrated Planning System (Monorepo)

High-Performance Enterprise Integrated Planning System with "Semantic Zoom" UX (Google Earth-style).

## Commands

```bash
bun i              # Install dependencies
bun run dev        # Start development server
bun run build      # Production build
bun run typecheck  # Type check all packages
bun run lint       # Lint all packages
bun run test       # Run tests
```

## Tech Stack

- **Runtime:** Bun
- **Monorepo:** Turborepo
- **App Framework:** Astro (Static/SPA, client-only React)
- **Frontend:** React 19
- **Graphics Engine:** @react-three/fiber, @react-three/drei, three-stdlib
- **State Management:** Custom state-machine (packages/toolkit)
- **Styling:** Tailwind CSS v4

## Project Structure

```
system/
├── apps/system/          # Main Astro application
├── packages/
│   ├── domain/           # Business logic, entities, data generators
│   ├── gl-engine/        # WebGL/R3F components, shaders, layers
│   ├── toolkit/          # State machine, utilities
│   └── ui/               # Atomic UI components
└── design/               # Visual reference specifications (Pixel-Perfect Target)
```

## Visual References (design/)

The `design/` directory contains the visual specifications that this system must replicate. See [design/README.md](./design/README.md) for detailed mapping between images and implementation layers.

| Image | View | Description |
|-------|------|-------------|
| screen-1.png | Macro | Strategic 10-20 year heatmap overview |
| screen-2.png | Meso | Tactical DNA-like allocation strips |
| screen-3.jpg | Micro | Operational task cards with dependencies |
| screen-4.png | Meso | Dense diagonal allocation pattern |
| screen-5.png | Meso | Color-coded resource allocation |
| screen-6.png | Micro | Task details with conflict indicators |
| screen-7.png | UI | Header/sidebar layout reference |

## LOD System (Level of Detail)

The system implements three rendering layers based on camera zoom:

- **Macro (zoom < 0.5):** Procedural shader heatmap - 1 draw call
- **Meso (0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0):** InstancedMesh with 60k instances
- **Micro (zoom > 2.0):** HTML overlay with Line2 dependency curves

## Documentation

- `docs/` - Domain documentation (requirements, UX specs, research)
- `agent_docs/` - Technical documentation for AI agents
- `design/` - Visual reference specifications

---

# Harness Protocol for Long-Running AI Agents

## What is a Harness?

A **harness** is a framework that enables AI agents to work effectively on complex tasks that span multiple sessions. Created by **Anthropic** as part of the Claude Agent SDK (formerly Claude Code SDK), the harness addresses a fundamental challenge:

> "The core challenge of long-running agents is that they must work in discrete sessions, and each new session begins with no memory of what came before."

The harness acts as a **coordination layer** wrapped around ordinary Claude sessions. It tracks files, tasks, and test results across hours of execution, spinning up fresh conversations whenever one thread gets too bloated to stay coherent.

## Why Anthropic Created the Harness

After more than six months shipping updates to Claude Code, Anthropic solved hard problems:
- How agents should manage **memory across long-running tasks**
- How to handle **permission systems** that balance autonomy with user control
- How to **coordinate subagents** working toward a shared goal

The key insight was finding a way for agents to quickly understand the state of work when starting with a fresh context window.

## Core Architecture

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    Harness Framework                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│                                                              │
│  ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐  │
│  │  Initializer │ → │    Coding    │ → │    Coding    │  │
│  │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │    │    Agent     │  │
│  │  (Session 1) │    │  (Session 2) │    │  (Session N) │  │
│  └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘  │
│         │                   │                   │           │
│         └───────────────────┼───────────────────┘           │
│                             │                               │
│                             ▼                               │
│  ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐  │
│  │              Shared State Files                       │  │
│  │  • claude-progress.txt  (session history)            │  │
│  │  • features.json        (source of truth)            │  │
│  │  • CLAUDE.md            (project context)            │  │
│  │  • git history          (code changes)               │  │
│  └──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘  │
│                                                              │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Two-Agent Architecture

Anthropic recommends a **two-fold approach**:

### 1. Initializer Agent (First Session)
- Sets up the initial environment
- Creates the harness files (features.json, claude-progress.txt)
- Establishes project structure
- Runs on a specialized prompt optimized for setup

### 2. Coding Agent (Subsequent Sessions)
- Makes incremental progress on features
- Leaves artifacts for the next session
- Maintains clean code suitable for merging
- Commits progress to git with descriptive messages

## Core Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `CLAUDE.md` | Project context, instructions, and conventions |
| `claude-progress.txt` | Session history and current state |
| `features.json` | Feature requirements with pass/fail status (source of truth) |
| `agent_docs/` | Additional documentation for the agent |
| `init.sh` | Script to start dev server and run basic tests |
| `CLAUDE_NOTES.md` | Learnings and gotchas for future sessions |

## The Work Loop

When a session starts, the agent follows this loop:

```
1. READ claude-progress.txt
   → Understand current state and what was done before

2. READ features.json
   → Identify next feature to implement (passes: false)

3. RUN init.sh
   → Start dev server, verify app is in working state

4. IMPLEMENT one feature at a time
   → Focus on single feature, don't try to do everything

5. TEST the feature
   → Verify it works as specified in steps

6. COMMIT changes
   → Granular commits with descriptive messages

7. UPDATE features.json
   → Mark feature as passes: true

8. UPDATE claude-progress.txt
   → Log what was done for next session

9. REPEAT or STOP
   → Continue to next feature or end session
```

## Critical AI Limitations & Mitigations

> **IMPORTANT**: AI agents have fundamental limitations that this harness addresses.

### The Blind Spot Problem

**AI cannot see what it creates.** When building UI/apps, the AI:
- Cannot see the rendered output
- Often assumes blank screens are "working" because code compiled
- Marks features as complete based on code logic, not visual verification
- May claim success when the app shows nothing or errors

**Solution**: Mandatory visual testing with MCP after every feature.

### The Stale Knowledge Problem

**AI training data is outdated.** Claude's knowledge cutoff means:
- Library APIs may have changed
- Configuration patterns may be deprecated
- New versions have different syntax
- Best practices may have evolved

**Solution**: Always check the current date and search for updated documentation.

### The Guessing Problem

**AI tends to guess instead of research.** When stuck, AI will:
- Try random solutions repeatedly
- Waste context on failed attempts
- Not realize when it's going in circles

**Solution**: Mandatory research protocol for non-obvious errors.

---

## Best Practices

### 1. One Feature at a Time
Don't attempt to complete everything at once. Focus on a single feature, test it, commit it, then move to the next.

### 2. Clean State Between Sessions
Leave the environment in a clean state:
- No major bugs
- Well-documented code
- Orderly structure
- Tests passing

### 3. features.json is Source of Truth
Never declare the project complete without all features passing. The `passes` field should only change from `false` to `true` after verification.

### 4. Commit After Each Feature
Maintain granular git history. Each feature should have its own commit(s).

### 5. Research Before Guessing
When encountering errors, classify them:
- **Simple errors** (typos, syntax): Fix directly
- **Complex errors** (runtime, config): Research online first

### 6. Test Before Marking Complete
Always verify the feature works before marking `passes: true`.

### 7. ALWAYS Check Current Date
Before starting work, verify the current date. **Do NOT assume you are in the year of your training data.** Use the system date to ensure you search for current documentation.

### 8. Install Dependencies via Commands
**NEVER manually edit package.json/Cargo.toml/requirements.txt to add dependencies.**

```bash
# CORRECT - Let package manager resolve versions
bun add react
npm install express
pip install requests
cargo add serde

# WRONG - Manual version specification
# Editing package.json: "react": "^18.0.0"  <- DON'T DO THIS
```

Why: Package managers install the latest stable compatible versions and handle peer dependencies correctly.

---

## MCP Visual Testing (MANDATORY)

### Why Visual Testing is Required

AI agents **cannot see rendered UI**. This creates a critical gap:

```
What AI knows:          What actually happens:
✓ Code compiled         ✗ Blank white screen
✓ No TypeScript errors  ✗ React error boundary
✓ Tests passed          ✗ Component doesn't render
✓ Server running        ✗ CSS completely broken
```

**Without visual verification, AI will mark broken features as complete.**

### Visual Testing Protocol

**See CLAUDE.md section "MCP VISUAL TESTING (MANDATORY)"** for the complete protocol.

Summary: Use **Playwright MCP** (headless, does not steal focus):

```
1. mcp__playwright__browser_navigate url="..."
2. mcp__playwright__browser_console_messages level="error"
3. mcp__playwright__browser_take_screenshot filename="..."
4. Analyze screenshot
5. mcp__playwright__browser_close
```

---

## Error Research Protocol

### When to Research vs. Fix Directly

| Error Type | Example | Action |
|------------|---------|--------|
| **Obvious** | Typo in variable name | Fix directly |
| **Syntax** | Missing semicolon | Fix directly |
| **Import** | Wrong import path | Fix directly |
| **Config** | Webpack/Vite config | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Runtime** | Module not found | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Library** | API changed | **RESEARCH FIRST** |
| **Unknown** | Cryptic error | **RESEARCH FIRST** |

### The STOP-SEARCH-UNDERSTAND-APPLY Method

When encountering non-obvious errors:

```
1. STOP
   - Do NOT try random solutions
   - Do NOT keep making changes hoping something works
   - Recognize when you're stuck

2. SEARCH
   - WebSearch: "[error message] + [technology] + [current year]"
   - WebFetch: Read official documentation
   - Include the CURRENT YEAR in searches (not your training year)

3. UNDERSTAND
   - Read the search results carefully
   - Identify the ROOT CAUSE
   - Understand WHY the error occurs

4. APPLY
   - Implement the CORRECT solution
   - Not a workaround or hack
   - The solution from documentation

5. DOCUMENT
   - Add to CLAUDE_NOTES.md if it's a recurring issue
   - Note in claude-progress.txt
```

### Example: Research Flow

```
Error: "Module '@tauri-apps/api/fs' not found"

❌ WRONG approach:
   → Try different import paths randomly
   → Downgrade versions blindly
   → Copy code from training data (outdated)

✅ CORRECT approach:
   1. STOP - This is a config/library error
   2. SEARCH - "tauri 2.0 fs module not found 2025"
   3. UNDERSTAND - Tauri 2.0 moved fs to plugin system
   4. APPLY - Install @tauri-apps/plugin-fs, update imports
   5. DOCUMENT - Note in CLAUDE_NOTES.md
```

---

## Session Startup Sequence

Every coding agent session should begin with:

```bash
# 1. Confirm working directory
pwd

# 2. Read progress to understand state
cat claude-progress.txt

# 3. Check recent git history
git log --oneline -10

# 4. Read feature requirements
cat features.json

# 5. Start development server
./init.sh

# 6. Verify app is working
# (run basic tests or visual check)

# 7. Begin work on next feature
```

## Context Management

The Claude Agent SDK implements automatic **compaction** when context limit approaches. However, you can help by:

- Using `/clear` between unrelated tasks
- Delegating research to subagents
- Keeping CLAUDE.md concise
- Writing clear, focused commit messages

## Results

Using this harness approach, Anthropic has observed:
- Claude maintaining focus for **30+ hours** on complex tasks
- Agents completing multi-week projects across sessions
- Consistent quality without context degradation

## Official Sources

- [Effective harnesses for long-running agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/effective-harnesses-for-long-running-agents)
- [Building agents with the Claude Agent SDK](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-agents-with-the-claude-agent-sdk)
- [Claude Code: Best practices for agentic coding](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/claude-code-best-practices)

1→# React + TypeScript + Vite
     2→
     3→This template provides a minimal setup to get React working in Vite with HMR and some ESLint rules.
     4→
     5→Currently, two official plugins are available:
     6→
     7→- [@vitejs/plugin-react](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/blob/main/packages/plugin-react) uses [Babel](https://babeljs.io/) (or [oxc](https://oxc.rs) when used in [rolldown-vite](https://vite.dev/guide/rolldown)) for Fast Refresh
     8→- [@vitejs/plugin-react-swc](https://github.com/vitejs/vite-plugin-react/blob/main/packages/plugin-react-swc) uses [SWC](https://swc.rs/) for Fast Refresh
     9→
    10→## React Compiler
    11→
    12→The React Compiler is not enabled on this template because of its impact on dev & build performances. To add it, see [this documentation](https://react.dev/learn/react-compiler/installation).
    13→
    14→## Expanding the ESLint configuration
    15→
    16→If you are developing a production application, we recommend updating the configuration to enable type-aware lint rules:
    17→
    18→```js
    19→export default defineConfig([
    20→  globalIgnores(['dist']),
    21→  {
    22→    files: ['**/*.{ts,tsx}'],
    23→    extends: [
    24→      // Other configs...
    25→
    26→      // Remove tseslint.configs.recommended and replace with this
    27→      tseslint.configs.recommendedTypeChecked,
    28→      // Alternatively, use this for stricter rules
    29→      tseslint.configs.strictTypeChecked,
    30→      // Optionally, add this for stylistic rules
    31→      tseslint.configs.stylisticTypeChecked,
    32→
    33→      // Other configs...
    34→    ],
    35→    languageOptions: {
    36→      parserOptions: {
    37→        project: ['./tsconfig.node.json', './tsconfig.app.json'],

file.content:
# Visual Design Specifications

This directory contains the **Pixel-Perfect Target** visual references for the Metro Integrated Planning System. Each image represents a specific view or component that must be replicated with high fidelity.

## View Mapping

### Macro View (Strategic Planning: 10-20 years)
**Implementation:** `packages/gl-engine/src/layers/MacroLayer.tsx`

| Reference | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `screen-1.png` | Dense grid/heatmap showing fleet density and "health" via color gradients (Yellow → Orange → Red) |

**Technical Requirements:**
- Single `PlaneGeometry` covering visible area
- Fragment Shader (GLSL) receiving `DataTexture`
- Color ramp: Yellow (low density) → Orange → Red (high density)
- Goal: 1 Draw Call, native GPU performance

---

### Meso View (Tactical Planning: 2-5 years)
**Implementation:** `packages/gl-engine/src/layers/MesoLayer.tsx`

| Reference | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `screen-2.png` | Colorful diagonal strips (DNA-like pattern) - high-density individual allocations |
| `screen-4.png` | Dense diagonal allocation pattern with interleaved colors |
| `screen-5.png` | Color-coded resource allocation blocks |

**Technical Requirements:**
- `InstancedMesh` with up to 60,000 instances
- Colors controlled via `InstancedBufferAttribute`
- Spatial Hash Grid for hover detection
- Color palette: Deep Blue (routine), Orange (overhaul)

---

### Micro View (Operational Planning: 4-8 weeks)
**Implementation:** `packages/gl-engine/src/layers/MicroLayer.tsx`

| Reference | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `screen-3.jpg` | Text boxes, alert icons, arrows showing task dependencies |
| `screen-6.png` | Task detail cards with conflict indicators (red alerts) |

**Technical Requirements:**
- `<Html>` component from drei with `transform` for task cards
- `Line2` (Fat Lines) for dependency arrows (smooth Bezier curves)
- Full Tailwind styling (shadows, rounded corners, flexbox)
- Accessibility: keyboard focus (tabIndex), native click events
- Conflict indicator: red border + warning icon

---

### UI Layout Reference
**Implementation:** `apps/system/src/components/PlanningView.tsx`

| Reference | Description |
|-----------|-------------|
| `screen-7.png` | Header bar + left sidebar (Train IDs) layout |

**Technical Requirements:**
- Header: Fleet availability %, budget status
- Left sidebar: Scrollable train list synchronized with canvas Y position
- Footer: Time scrubber / Minimap controlling canvas X position

---

## Color Palette

| Element | Color | Hex |
|---------|-------|-----|
| Background | Soft corporate gray | `#f4f4f5` (zinc-100) |
| Task (routine) | Deep Blue | `#1d4ed8` (blue-700) |
| Task (overhaul) | Orange | `#f59e0b` (amber-500) |
| Alert/Conflict | Danger Red | `#dc2626` (red-600) |
| Dependency lines | Slate | `#64748b` (slate-500) |

---

## Implementation Checklist

- [x] MacroLayer: Procedural shader heatmap
- [x] MesoLayer: InstancedMesh with spatial hashing
- [x] MicroLayer: HTML overlay + Line2 curves
- [x] Virtual scroll sync (sidebar ↔ canvas)
- [x] Time scrubber minimap
- [ ] Pixel-perfect color matching
- [ ] Fine-tuning visual density patterns

content.content:
1→# WebGL Performance Patterns for High-Density Planning Systems
     2→
     3→Reference for building CAD/Google Earth-style applications in the browser with 50k+ objects.
     4→
     5→Source: Expert patterns for systems like Figma, Google Maps, professional CAD tools.
     6→
     7→---
     8→
     9→## 1. GPU Data Patterns
    10→
    11→### Data Textures (State as Texture)
    12→
    13→**What:** Encode object state (color, status, conflict) in a `DataTexture` instead of CPU loops.
    14→
    15→**Why:** Updating 50k object colors via CPU = 50k operations. Uploading a 256x256 texture = 1 operation.
    16→
    17→**How:**
    18→```javascript
    19→// Create texture once
    20→const data = new Uint8Array(width * height * 4); // RGBA
    21→const texture = new DataTexture(data, width, height, RGBAFormat);
    22→
    23→// Update state (CPU)
    24→data[index * 4 + 0] = red;
    25→data[index * 4 + 1] = green;
    26→texture.needsUpdate = true;
    27→
    28→// Read in shader (GPU)
    29→vec4 state = texture2D(uStateTexture, vec2(instanceId / width, 0.0));
    30→```
    31→
    32→**Trigger:** When you have >1000 objects that need state updates per frame.
    33→
    34→**Already in project:** MacroLayer heatmap uses this pattern.
    35→
    36→---
    37→
    38→### Struct of Arrays (SoA) vs Array of Structs (AoS)
    39→
    40→**What:** Store data in parallel typed arrays instead of object arrays.
    41→
    42→**Why:**
    43→- Cache-friendly memory access
    44→- Direct upload to GPU buffers
    45→- No GC pressure from object allocation
    46→
    47→**Example:**
    48→```javascript
    49→// AoS (bad for GPU)
    50→const tasks = [{ id: 1, x: 10, y: 20 }, { id: 2, x: 15, y: 25 }];
    51→
    52→// SoA (GPU-friendly)
    53→const ids = new Uint32Array([1, 2]);
    54→const positions = new Float32Array([10, 20, 15, 25]); // x1,y1,x2,y2
    55→```
    56→
    57→**Trigger:** When JSON parsing or array operations become bottleneck (profile first).
    58→
    59→**Note:** Adds complexity. Only use when measured performance requires it.
    60→
    61→---
    62→
    63→## 2. Picking & Interaction
    64→
    65→### Math-Based Picking (O(1) vs Raycaster O(N))
    66→
    67→**What:** For grid-based layouts (Gantt, timeline), calculate hit directly from mouse position.
    68→
    69→**Why:** Three.js Raycaster checks every object. Math is instant.
    70→
    71→**How:**
    72→```javascript
    73→// For time-based grid
    74→const day = Math.floor((mouseX / zoom) + offsetX) / DAY_WIDTH;
    75→const train = Math.floor((mouseY / zoom) + offsetY) / ROW_HEIGHT;
    76→const taskId = lookupGrid[train][day]; // O(1)
    77→```
    78→
    79→**Trigger:** When hover/click feels sluggish with many objects.
    80→
    81→**Already in project:** MesoLayer uses Spatial Hash Grid for this.
    82→
    83→---
    84→
    85→### Spatial Hash Grid
    86→
    87→**What:** Divide space into cells, store object references per cell.
    88→
    89→**How:**
    90→```javascript
    91→const cellSize = 50; // pixels
    92→const grid = new Map();
    93→
    94→function addToGrid(obj) {
    95→  const key = `${Math.floor(obj.x / cellSize)}_${Math.floor(obj.y / cellSize)}`;
    96→  if (!grid.has(key)) grid.set(key, []);
    97→  grid.get(key).push(obj);
    98→}
    99→
   100→function queryPoint(x, y) {
   101→  const key = `${Math.floor(x / cellSize)}_${Math.floor(y / cellSize)}`;
   102→  return grid.get(key) || [];
   103→}
   104→```
   105→
   106→**Trigger:** When you need fast spatial queries (hover detection, collision).
   107→
   108→---
   109→
   110→### Pointer Events Layering
   111→
   112→**What:** Control which layer receives mouse events.
   113→
   114→**Why:** HTML overlays steal events from canvas underneath.
   115→
   116→**How:**
   117→```css
   118→.overlay-container { pointer-events: none; }
   119→.overlay-container .clickable-card { pointer-events: auto; }
   120→```
   121→
   122→**Trigger:** When clicks don't reach the canvas, or tooltips block interaction.
   123→
   124→---
   125→
   126→## 3. LOD (Level of Detail) Transitions
   127→
   128→### Hysteresis (Dead Zone)
   129→
   130→**What:** Use different thresholds for entering vs leaving a LOD level.
   131→
   132→**Why:** Without it, user zooming near threshold causes flickering.
   133→
   134→**How:**
   135→```javascript
   136→// Bad: flickers at exactly 0.5
   137→const lod = zoom > 0.5 ? 'meso' : 'macro';
   138→
   139→// Good: stable transition
   140→let currentLod = 'macro';
   141→if (currentLod === 'macro' && zoom > 0.55) currentLod = 'meso';
   142→if (currentLod === 'meso' && zoom < 0.45) currentLod = 'macro';
   143→```
   144→
   145→**Trigger:** Implement from the start when building LOD systems.
   146→
   147→---
   148→
   149→### Opacity Crossfade
   150→
   151→**What:** Fade between LOD layers instead of hard switch.
   152→
   153→**How:**
   154→```javascript
   155→const t = smoothstep(0.45, 0.55, zoom);
   156→macroLayer.opacity = 1 - t;
   157→mesoLayer.opacity = t;
   158→```
   159→
   160→**Trigger:** When LOD transitions feel jarring.
   161→
   162→---
   163→
   164→## 4. React + WebGL Performance
   165→
   166→### React-Free Zone
   167→
   168→**What:** Bypass React for 60fps operations.
   169→
   170→**Why:** useState triggers re-render. At 60fps = 60 re-renders/second = death.
   171→
   172→**How:**
   173→```javascript
   174→// Bad: React re-renders 60x/sec
   175→const [position, setPosition] = useState({ x: 0, y: 0 });
   176→useFrame(() => setPosition({ x: mouse.x, y: mouse.y }));
   177→
   178→// Good: Direct mutation, no re-render
   179→const positionRef = useRef({ x: 0, y: 0 });
   180→useFrame(() => {
   181→  positionRef.current.x = mouse.x;
   182→  mesh.current.position.x = positionRef.current.x;
   183→});
   184→```
   185→
   186→**Trigger:** Any animation or continuous interaction (drag, pan, zoom, hover highlight).
   187→
   188→---
   189→
   190→### Object Pooling (GC Avoidance)
   191→
   192→**What:** Reuse objects instead of creating new ones in hot paths.
   193→
   194→**Why:** `new Vector3()` in useFrame = 60 allocations/sec = GC stutters.
   195→
   196→**How:**
   197→```javascript
   198→// Bad: creates garbage
   199→useFrame(() => {
   200→  const temp = new Vector3(x, y, z);
   201→  mesh.position.copy(temp);
   202→});
   203→
   204→// Good: reuse
   205→const temp = useMemo(() => new Vector3(), []);
   206→useFrame(() => {
   207→  temp.set(x, y, z);
   208→  mesh.position.copy(temp);
   209→});
   210→```
   211→
   212→**Trigger:** Any code inside useFrame, event handlers, or animation loops.
   213→
   214→---
   215→
   216→## 5. Numerical Precision
   217→
   218→### Local Coordinates
   219→
   220→**What:** Subtract a global offset from large numbers before sending to GPU.
   221→
   222→**Why:** Float32 has ~7 digits precision. Unix timestamp (1700000000000) overflows.
   223→
   224→**How:**
   225→```javascript
   226→const EPOCH = new Date('2024-01-01').getTime();
   227→
   228→// CPU: convert to local
   229→const localTime = (task.timestamp - EPOCH) / MS_PER_DAY; // small number
   230→
   231→// Send to shader
   232→uniforms.uTime.value = localTime;
   233→```
   234→
   235→**Trigger:** When objects wobble or disappear at high zoom. When using timestamps in shaders.
   236→
   237→---
   238→
   239→### Z-Fighting Prevention
   240→
   241→**What:** Add micro-offset to Z position for overlapping planes.
   242→
   243→**Why:** Same Z + depth buffer = flickering.
   244→
   245→**How:**
   246→```javascript
   247→// In 2D orthographic view
   248→mesh.position.z = layerIndex * 0.001;
   249→// or
   250→mesh.renderOrder = layerIndex;
   251→```
   252→
   253→**Trigger:** When overlapping elements flicker.
   254→
   255→---
   256→
   257→## 6. Loading & Data
   258→
   259→### Progressive Loading
   260→
   261→**What:** Load visible data first, fetch more on demand.
   262→
   263→**Why:** 10 years of detailed data = huge payload. User sees 1 month at a time.
   264→
   265→**Pattern:**
   266→```
   267→1. Load viewport + buffer (current view ± 20%)
   268→2. On pan/zoom, fetch missing chunks
   269→3. Macro view = aggregated data (small)
   270→4. Micro view = detailed data (load on demand)
   271→```
   272→
   273→**Trigger:** When initial load is slow or payload > 1MB.
   274→
   275→---
   276→
   277→### Virtualización Lógica
   278→
   279→**What:** Only process visible data range, not entire dataset.
   280→
   281→**How:**
   282→```javascript
   283→const visibleStart = Math.floor(cameraX / DAY_WIDTH);
   284→const visibleEnd = Math.ceil((cameraX + viewportWidth) / DAY_WIDTH);
   285→
   286→// Only iterate visible range
   287→for (let i = visibleStart; i < visibleEnd; i++) {
   288→  renderTask(tasks[i]);
   289→}
   290→```
   291→
   292→**Trigger:** When you have 10+ years of data but show weeks at a time.
   293→
   294→---
   295→
   296→## 7. Debugging Tools
   297→
   298→### stats.gl
   299→
   300→**What:** Performance monitor showing FPS, draw calls, triangles, GPU memory.
   301→
   302→**Why:** stats.js only shows FPS. You need to know WHY it's slow.
   303→
   304→**Install:** `npm install stats.gl`
   305→
   306→---
   307→
   308→### Spector.js
   309→
   310→**What:** Chrome extension that captures WebGL frames.
   311→
   312→**Why:** Shows exactly what GPU drew, shader by shader. Essential for "why is this invisible?" bugs.
   313→
   314→**Install:** Chrome Web Store → Spector.js
   315→
   316→---
   317→
   318→### Leva
   319→
   320→**What:** Runtime GUI for tweaking values.
   321→
   322→**Why:** Change colors, thresholds, sizes without code reload.
   323→
   324→**Use:** During development, expose magic numbers as Leva controls.
   325→
   326→---
   327→
   328→### Debug Render Mode
   329→
   330→**What:** Alternate coloring that shows render cost or instance index.
   331→
   332→**Why:** Visually identifies objects being rendered that shouldn't be (offscreen, hidden).
   333→
   334→**How:**
   335→```javascript
   336→// Toggle with Alt+D
   337→if (debugMode) {
   338→  color = instanceIndex / totalInstances; // gradient by index
   339→  // or
   340→  color = isOffscreen ? RED : GREEN;
   341→}
   342→```
   343→
   344→**Trigger:** When FPS is low but you don't know what's causing it.
   345→
   346→---
   347→
   348→## 8. UX Patterns
   349→
   350→### Ghost Drag
   351→
   352→**What:** Show semi-transparent copy during drag, keep original in place.
   353→
   354→**Why:** User sees immediate feedback + can cancel safely.
   355→
   356→**Trigger:** When implementing drag-and-drop for task rescheduling.
   357→
   358→---
   359→
   360→### Scroll Sync (Canvas as Source)
   361→
   362→**What:** WebGL camera position drives HTML sidebar scroll, not vice versa.
   363→
   364→**Why:** Canvas animation is smoother. HTML following canvas = smooth. Canvas following HTML = janky.
   365→
   366→**How:**
   367→```javascript
   368→// In animation loop
   369→sidebarElement.style.transform = `translateY(${-cameraY * PX_PER_TRAIN}px)`;
   370→```
   371→
   372→**Already in project:** Implemented in TrainList.tsx
   373→
   374→---
   375→
   376→## 9. Shader Patterns
   377→
   378→### Shader Warm-up
   379→
   380→**What:** Force shader compilation before user interaction.
   381→
   382→**Why:** First render with new shader = compilation jank (freeze).
   383→
   384→**How:**
   385→```javascript
   386→// Before showing UI
   387→renderer.compile(scene, camera);
   388→// or render one invisible frame
   389→```
   390→
   391→**Trigger:** When first interaction causes visible freeze.
   392→
   393→---
   394→
   395→### Discard vs Alpha
   396→
   397→**What:** In fragment shader, `discard` vs `alpha = 0.0`.
   398→
   399→**Why:** `discard` removes from depth buffer (good for holes). Alpha keeps in buffer.
   400→
   401→**Trigger:** When transparent objects behave unexpectedly with depth.
   402→
   403→---
   404→
   405→## Quick Reference: When to Apply
   406→
   407→| Symptom | Pattern to Apply |
   408→|---------|------------------|
   409→| Slow state updates (color changes) | Data Textures |
   410→| Slow hover/click detection | Math Picking / Spatial Hash |
   411→| LOD transition flickers | Hysteresis + Crossfade |
   412→| Animation stutters | React-Free Zone + Object Pooling |
   413→| Objects wobble at high zoom | Local Coordinates |
   414→| Overlapping elements flicker | Z-Fighting prevention |
   415→| Initial load slow | Progressive Loading |
   416→| FPS low, unknown cause | Debug Render Mode + stats.gl |
   417→| Clicks not reaching canvas | Pointer Events layering |
   418→

// light yellow 60→ vec3 b = vec3(1.0, 0.6, 0.0); // orange 61→ vec3 c = vec3(0.85, 0.0, 0.0); // red 62→ return mix(mix(a, b, smoothstep(0.0, 0.5, t)), c, smoothstep(0.5, 1.0, t)); 63→} 64→``` 65→ 66→--- 67→ 68→### Bun Workspaces / Monorepo 69→ 70→#### Key Learning 71→Bun workspaces require proper package.json structure and cross-package imports. 72→ 73→#### Important Configuration 74→```json 75→{ 76→ "workspaces": ["packages/*", "apps/*"] 77→} 78→``` 79→ 80→#### CRITICAL: Running Scripts in Monorepos 81→ 82→**The root `bun run dev` may fail** if the filter doesn't match the package name: 83→ 84→```bash 85→# Root package.json uses filter: 86→"dev": "bun --bun run --filter '@mms/app-system' dev" 87→ 88→# The filter MUST match the 'name' field in apps/system/package.json: 89→"name": "@mms/app-system" # ← This is what the filter matches 90→``` 91→ 92→**If root scripts fail, ALWAYS try the direct approach:** 93→ 94→```bash 95→# This ALWAYS works: 96→cd apps/system && bun run dev 97→ 98→# Or check what the package is actually named: 99→cat apps/system/package.json | grep '"name"' 100→``` 101→ 102→#### Common Pitfalls 103→- **Never assume root scripts work** - verify first with `./init.sh --no-server` 104→- Root scripts use filters that must match exact package names 105→- Always use `bun add` from the correct directory 106→- Cross-package imports need proper exports in package.json 107→- Run `bun i` from root after adding new packages 108→ 109→--- 110→ 111→## Package/Library Notes 112→ 113→### @react-three/drei 114→ 115→#### IMPORTANT: Html Component 116→ 117→**Correct import**: `import { Html } from '@react-three/drei'` 118→ 119→#### Why this matters 120→The Html component bridges WebGL and DOM, allowing real HTML elements to be positioned in 3D space. 121→ 122→#### Correct usage 123→```tsx 124→ 130→
131→ Clickable content 132→
133→ 134→``` 135→ 136→--- 137→ 138→### three-stdlib 139→ 140→#### IMPORTANT: Line2 for Fat Lines 141→ 142→**Correct import**: `import { Line2, LineGeometry, LineMaterial } from 'three-stdlib'` 143→ 144→#### Why this matters 145→Standard Three.js Line has 1px width limit. Line2 supports variable width. 146→ 147→#### Correct usage 148→```typescript 149→const geometry = new LineGeometry(); 150→geometry.setPositions([x1, y1, z1, x2, y2, z2]); 151→ 152→const material = new LineMaterial({ 153→ color: 0x3b82f6, 154→ linewidth: 2, 155→ resolution: new Vector2(window.innerWidth, window.innerHeight) 156→}); 157→``` 158→ 159→--- 160→ 161→## Error Solutions 162→ 163→### Error: "No packages matched the filter" (Bun monorepo) 164→**Cause**: Root script uses `--filter` with wrong package name 165→**Solution**: 166→1. Check actual package name: `cat apps/system/package.json | grep '"name"'` 167→2. Run directly in the app folder: `cd apps/system && bun run dev` 168→3. Or fix the filter in root package.json to match the actual package name 169→ 170→### Error: "Cannot use import statement outside a module" 171→**Cause**: Package trying to use CommonJS in ESM context 172→**Solution**: Ensure `"type": "module"` in package.json, use proper imports 173→ 174→### Error: "useFrame must be called inside Canvas" 175→**Cause**: React hook called outside R3F context 176→**Solution**: Move component inside `` or pass state via props/context 177→ 178→### Error: "THREE.WebGLRenderer: Context Lost" 179→**Cause**: GPU memory exhausted or too many textures 180→**Solution**: Dispose unused textures, reduce DataTexture size, check for memory leaks 181→ 182→### Error: InstancedMesh colors not updating 183→**Cause**: Buffer attribute not marked for update 184→**Solution**: Set `mesh.instanceColor!.needsUpdate = true` after changing colors 185→ 186→--- 187→ 188→## General Rules 189→ 190→1. **Always research official documentation** before trying to resolve import/ESM errors 191→2. **Check for alternative versions** of packages for different bundlers 192→3. **Don't remove dependencies** the user explicitly requested - find the correct way to use them 193→4. **Test after every change** - don't batch multiple changes 194→5. **Commit frequently** - small, focused commits are better than large ones 195→6. **Use bun add** - never manually edit package.json for dependencies 196→ 197→--- 198→ 199→## Project-Specific Gotchas 200→ 201→### LOD State Machine 202→The LOD (Level of Detail) is controlled by zoom level: 203→- `zoom < 0.5` → 'macro' (shader heatmap) 204→- `0.5 ≤ zoom ≤ 2.0` → 'meso' (InstancedMesh) 205→- `zoom > 2.0` → 'micro' (HTML overlay) 206→ 207→### Virtual Scroll Sync 208→Sidebar scroll is synced with camera Y using: 209→```typescript 210→const PX_PER_TRAIN = 24; 211→// DOM → Camera 212→const y = -el.scrollTop / PX_PER_TRAIN; 213→// Camera → DOM 214→el.scrollTop = -viewport.y * PX_PER_TRAIN; 215→``` 216→ 217→### Spatial Hash Grid 218→MesoLayer uses spatial hashing for O(1) instance picking: 219→- Grid cell size matches task dimensions 220→- Hash key: `${Math.floor(x / cellSize)}_${Math.floor(y / cellSize)}` 221→ 222→--- 223→ 224→## Useful Commands 225→ 226→```bash 227→# Install all workspace dependencies 228→bun i 229→ 230→# Start development server 231→bun run dev 232→ 233→# Type check all packages 234→bun run typecheck 235→ 236→# Build for production 237→bun run build 238→ 239→# Check git status 240→git status 241→ 242→# View recent commits 243→git --no-pager log --oneline -10 244→``` 245→ 246→--- 247→ 248→## Resources 249→ 250→- [React Three Fiber Docs](https://docs.pmnd.rs/react-three-fiber) 251→- [Drei Docs](https://github.com/pmndrs/drei) 252→- [Three.js Docs](https://threejs.org/docs/) 253→- [Astro Docs](https://docs.astro.build) 254→- [Bun Docs](https://bun.sh/docs) 255→- [Tailwind CSS v4](https://tailwindcss.com/docs) 256→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware.
